coredata.dc:title: Application of carbon dots as efficient catalyst for the green oxidation of phenol: Kinetic study of the degradation and optimization using response surface methodology
coredata.dc:creator: Pirsaheb, Meghdad
Moradi, Sajad
Shahlaei, Mohsen
Farhadian, Negin
coredata.dc:description: The reactivity of bio-polymer based nano carbon dots (NCDs) was studied for catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 to generate free hydroxyl radicals and consequently its applicability in the removal of phenol. To the best of our knowledge, for the first time, this work reports that bio-polymer based NCDs could activate H2O2 and yielding more than 99% phenol degradation within 20 min in the presence of 12 mmol H2O2. Herein, a simple hydrothermal carbonization route was employed for the synthesis of NCDs. The surface morphology, functional groups and crystallinity of the NCDs are studied. SEM images show the NCDs with spherical shape. The graphitic nature of the NCDs was evident from the XRD pattern. The presence of many surface functional groups is confirmed by FT-IR spectra. The influences of three independent operating parameters involving hydrogen peroxide concentration (4–12 mmol), reaction time (10–20 min) and catalyst amount (50–1000 ppm) on the phenol oxidation rate by two NCDs were examined using response surface methodology (RSM). Terephthalic acid (TA) dosimetry demonstrates that the as-prepared NCDs can produce hydroxyl radicals from H2O2, and hence catalyze the oxidation of phenol. Finally, the reusability of the NCDs catalysts was examined and the produced catalysts exhibit good recyclability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots catalysts
Degradation efficiency
H2O2
Phenol
Response surface methodology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for the preparation of NCDs
Hydroxyl radical detection by TA dosimetry
Evaluation of catalytic degradation of phenol
Optimization of the catalytic degradation experiments
Effect of pH on the degradation efficiency of phenol
Hydroxyl radical generation during catalytic experiment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for NCDA and NCDB samples.
FTIR spectra for NCDA and NCDB samples.
SEM images belongs to a) NCDA and b) NCDB.
Normal plot of residual (a, b) and predicted versus actual values (c, d).
Response surface plots for degradation of phenol using NCDA and NCDB catalysts showing interaction between process parameters.
Effect of pH on the degradation efficiency of phenol (H2O2 dosage of 12 mmol, time of 19 min and amount of catalyst of 1000 ppm).
Florescence spectra of NCDA and NCDB.
Recycling results of the degradation experiments using two catalysts after four runs (Reaction conditions: catalyst loading = 1000 ppm, H2O2 dosage = 12 mmol, time = 19 min).
First-order reaction kinetics for the degradation of phenol using NCDA and NCDB catalysts (Reaction conditions: catalyst loading = 1000 ppm, H2O2 dosage = 8 mmol).

The degradation performance of NCDA catalyst (initial concentration of phenol = 50 ppm, H2O2 dosage = 12 mmol, catalyst loading = 1000 ppm). Peaks specified by 1–3 are belongs to hydroquinone, catechol and phenol, respectively.
The time dependence disappearance of phenol and appearance of aromatic intermediates during catalytic hydroxylation reaction (catalyst loading = 1000 ppm, H2O2 dosage = 12 mmol).
Independent process parameters and their levels.
Experimental design matrix of the CCD for degradation process.
Comparison of the predicted and observed degradation efficiencies.
ANOVA results for both process responses.

head.ce:title: Application of carbon dots as efficient catalyst for the green oxidation of phenol: Kinetic study of the degradation and optimization using response surface methodology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Environmental Determinants of Health (RCEDH), School of Public Health, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Environmental Determinants of Health (RCEDH)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, School of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical, Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Environmental Health Engineering, School of Public Health, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The reactivity of bio-polymer based nano carbon dots (NCDs) was studied for catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 to generate free hydroxyl radicals and consequently its applicability in the removal of phenol. To the best of our knowledge, for the first time, this work reports that bio-polymer based NCDs could activate H2O2 and yielding more than 99% phenol degradation within 20 min in the presence of 12 mmol H2O2. Herein, a simple hydrothermal carbonization route was employed for the synthesis of NCDs. The surface morphology, functional groups and crystallinity of the NCDs are studied. SEM images show the NCDs with spherical shape. The graphitic nature of the NCDs was evident from the XRD pattern. The presence of many surface functional groups is confirmed by FT-IR spectra. The influences of three independent operating parameters involving hydrogen peroxide concentration (4–12 mmol), reaction time (10–20 min) and catalyst amount (50–1000 ppm) on the phenol oxidation rate by two NCDs were examined using response surface methodology (RSM). Terephthalic acid (TA) dosimetry demonstrates that the as-prepared NCDs can produce hydroxyl radicals from H2O2, and hence catalyze the oxidation of phenol. Finally, the reusability of the NCDs catalysts was examined and the produced catalysts exhibit good recyclability.

ce:section.ce:para: Urbanization, population expansion and developments in industrialization can create huge amount of pollutants in which discharged mainly to the water body and consequently generates public concern. Among the different type of aqueous pollutants exist in wastewater, organic compounds creates fatal hazards to human health. Also, some of them are persistent in the eco-environment and are resistant toward natural degradation. In this regard, many efforts have been devoted on exploring new ways to reaching the complete decomposition of organic pollutants. Phenol, a common organic pollutant in industrial discharges mostly has been taken as a model component for wastewater treatment [1]. Phenol and its derivatives are discharged in the environment through different industries including: oil refining, pharmaceutical, and petrochemical, polymer, coking, iron-smelting, fiberglass plants, dye and metallurgical operations [2–7]. Reducing and removing phenol and organic pollutants from wastewaters and drinking waters is an important task which may be performed by various biological [8], chemical [9–12], physical and advanced oxidation process (AOPs) [13–16]. Recently an introduction of a new low cost, abundant and high efficiency catalyst material with considerable ability in fully degrading of phenol is very encouraged.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new category of carbon materials are produced based on ultrasonication, microwave and hydrothermal methods [17–19]. CDs have attracted many interests as a result of their simple synthetic routes, excellent water solubility, low cost and toxicity, high photo stability, ease of functionalization and superior biocompatibility [20–23]. They are employed in various fields including drug delivery, sensing, bioimaging, light emitting devices, catalysis, and environmental remediation/monitoring [24–27]. Also, recently, CDs with the unique fluorescent and photo-electrochemical fascinating properties can serve the electron reservoir/transfer ability as a photo-induced electron donors or acceptors that mainly introduced the CDs as an efficient photocatalyst [21,28]. Also, in order to enhance wider spectrum separation and absorption of electron-hole and also stabilize photolysis semiconductors, CDs are applied as a multi-functional compound in design of high-performance photocatalysts [23,29]. For example, in the green oxidation of cyclohexane to cyclohexanone in the presence of H2O2 at room temperature under visible light, the Au nanoparticles/CDs photocatalyst yields 99.9% selectivity and 63.8% conversion efficiency [30]. Li et al. [31] prepared a ZnO/CD composite structure using a sol–gel method and the prepared composite exhibit promoted photocatalytic activity. Moreover, composites constructed from CD and semiconductors such as CDs/SiO2, CDs/Fe2O3, CDs/ Ag3PO4, CDs/TiO2 and CDs/Cu2O have showed good photocatalytic activities [29,32–35].

Moreover, Han et al. reported that CDs are good H-bonding catalysts because they have excellent photochemical characteristics as well as functional hydroxyl and carboxylic groups [36]. The Nitrogen doped CDs show high catalytic activity for the reduction of methylene blue using NaBH4 [37]. Due to the prominent photoluminescence properties of the CDs, these materials are often used as photocatalyst and the catalytic ability of these materials has been less considered. Regarding these lack of information, for the first time, the ability of CDs to activation of hydrogen peroxide was examined. In this work, the green synthesized bio-polymer based NCDs were used as homogeneous catalysts for the degradation of phenol in the presence of H2O2. The bio-friendly NCDs simply prepared by hydrothermal carbonization of carbohydrate based materials (Gum Tragacanth (GT) and Chitosan) as natural precursors. The surface functional groups, morphology and crystallinity of them were determined by using various experimental techniques. Furthermore, central composite design (CCD) approach as a type of response surface methodology (RSM) was implemented to design the experiments and develop models in order to elucidate the relationships among considered operating parameters and process responses [38–40]. The reactivity of as-prepared NCDs towards H2O2 activation and generation of highly reactive hydroxyl radical was examined using Terephtalic acid chemical dosimetry. This work is aimed to introduce a new class of low cost, simple constructed carbon materials as an efficient catalyst with high conversion and selectivity for the decomposition of organic contaminants in nature with the advantages of no excessive production of wastes.

The raw GT was collected from plants growing naturally within in different regions of Iran. The sodium phosphate, phenol (99%), hydroquinone, catechol, methanol, acetonitrile, acetic acid, medium molecular weight Chitosan, ethanol (98%) and Trephthalic acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. H2O2 (30% w/w) was obtained from Merck, Germany.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the preparation of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: NCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization technique. For this purpose, initially, an appropriate amount (10 g) of GT was grounded by a mixer grinder to form a powder, and then GT solution (1% w/w) was prepared by dissolving clear GT powder in deionized water, kept at room temperature overnight. Similarly, the chitosan solution in acetic acid (1% v/v) was obtained. The pure GT solution and an equal volume ratio of GT and chitosan solutions were used for the preparation of NCDs. At first, a defined volume of GT solution and ethanol were mixed and transferred into a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. Once again, certain amounts of Chitosan, GT and ethanol were mixed together and poured to the autoclave. The mixture was kept at 180 °C for 2 h. Then, the autoclave was cooled and the product was filtered using filter paper and washed with dichloromethane to eliminate unreacted compounds. Finally, after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant was collected and kept at 4 °C for further analysis. The carbon dot based on GT/Chitosan is named NCDA and other one named NCDB.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra for the bio-polymer based NCDs were recorded using a Shimadzu FTIR spectrophotometer (Japan) (FT-IR-8300) at room temperature. The surface morphology of the both types of NCDs was visualized through scanning electron microscopy (Shimadzu, Japan) (SEM). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer LS 45 Fluorescence spectrometer. Crystallinity of the both NCDA and NCDB samples was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns with a Inel Equaniox 3000, France, 30 kV, 20 mA, K alpha Cu:1.54 Å.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydroxyl radical detection by TA dosimetry
ce:section.ce:para: In order to show if the OH radicals are generated from the catalytic decomposition of H2O2, TA dosimetry was carried out. A solution containing 10 mM TA in a 10 mM sodium phosphate buffer was prepared and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.5. After addition of specified amounts of catalysts and H2O2, the fluorescence of the solution was measured using a Fluorescence spectrometer with at emission wavelength at 425 nm and an excitation at 315 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of catalytic degradation of phenol
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the catalytic activity of the two synthesized NCDs for the degradation of phenol, the catalytic oxidation experiments were performed by a face centered central composite design of experiment (CCD) defined under RSM. The three independent parameters of process (hydrogen peroxide dosage, reaction time and catalyst amount) were chosen with the degradation of phenol using NCDA and NCDB as two response variables. High and low levels of the three parameters are presented in Table 1 . The experimental design was done using Design Expert Software (ver. 7). Table 2 shows the experimental runs designed under RSM. Based on Table 2, batch degradation runs were carried out in a 50 ml reactor containing 50 ppm of phenol solution at ambient pressure and temperature. The pH of the solution was adjusted to approximately 3 using HCl (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M). The different dosages of the NCDs were added to the solution under continuous stirring. Then, a defined amount of the H2O2 was added and allowed to continuously stirred. At defined reaction times, 2 ml aliquot of the solution was taken out and the reaction was quenched by adding methanol. Phenol concentration was determined by a high-performance liquid chromatography equipped with a UV-detector set at 270 nm under isocratic mode with acetonitrile and water (20:80% v/v). All experiments were done twice and the average value was reported. The degradation efficiency of phenol was estimated using the following formula:
(1)
Y 1
=
C 0
-
C f
C f
Where Y1 is the degradation efficiency (%) of phenol after exposure to catalyst NCDA, C0 is the initial concentration of phenol (ppm) and Cf is the final concentration of phenol after reaction time of t based on ppm. Similarly, the Y2 is defined as the degradation efficiency (%) of phenol after exposure to catalyst NCDB and with the difference that the concentrations in the Eq. (1) are related to the amount of phenol exposed to the catalyst NCDB.

The complete phenol degradation using H2O2 has been investigated based on the following equation [41]:
(2)
C 6
H 6
O + 14
H 2
O 2
→ 6 C
O 2
+ 17
H 2
O

As it can be seen, the stoichiometric molar ratio of phenol and H2O2 is 1:14. In this regard, for completer degradation of 50 ppm phenol approximately 7.4 mmol of H2O2 is demanded. As a result of that, 4–12 mmol of H2O2 was added.

It should be noted that initially five parameters (H2O2 dosage, reaction time, amount of catalyst, initial pH and initial concentration of phenol) were considered. To save time and money and to reduce the number of experiments, three of them entered into the experimental design process, and the others were examined separately. The effect of initial pH was investigated under the optimal conditions determined for other variables and the effect of changing the concentration of phenol was also discussed in the kinetic study.

Fig. 1 illustrates XRD patterns of the synthesized bio-polymer based NCDs. From XRD pattern of NCDB sample a broad peak observed at 2θ = 18° indicate the graphitic nature of the samples and could be assigned to disturbances of non-repeatable side chains in GT structure. But in the case of the XRD patterns for the NCDA sample, two peaks at 2θ = 23° & 32°, are observed. It should be noted that Chitosan has helped GT to make bigger crystallite as in the NCDA XRD pattern the broad peak related to GT didn’t observed. In the other hand according to brag equation smaller distance of crystal planes appeared in bigger 2θ. As can be seen in Fig. 1 the peaks related to NCDA are in the right of those of NCDB samples that can be because of more compact structure in GT/chitosan dots.

Fig. 2 displays the FT-IR spectra of the both samples. The NCDA catalysts shows absorption bands at 3410, 2927, 1624, 1577 and 1053 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching frequency of OH, CH, CO, NH, COC functional groups, respectively. For the NCDB samples the characteristic absorption peaks at 3414, 2924, 1215, 1049 cm−1 are corresponds to OH, CH, COC and CN stretching vibrations, respectively.

Surface morphology and structure of the as-prepared NCDA and NCDB samples were studied by SEM images, as shown in Fig. 3 . From the SEM images, the average particle size for NCDA and NCDB particles is 65 nm and 40 nm, respectively. Also SEM images exhibits that the NCDA and NCDB nanoparticles are mono-disperse and spherical.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the catalytic degradation experiments
ce:section.ce:para: NCDs, a new practically acceptable and ecofriendly catalysts were applied to decomposing H2O2 and generate hydroxyl radicals from it. Initially, the apparent ability of NCDs catalysts for phenol absorption was examined in the system containing only catalysts. When only catalysts were present, little absorption was occurred and indicating that the catalysts alone were not capable to remove high amounts of phenol. In contrast, high amount of phenol degradation was observed in the system involving both catalyst and H2O2. For the system containing pure catalysts, the maximum phenol removal was only close to 23% which happened due to absorption, but the degradation efficiency of phenol at the presence of both H2O2 and catalysts was significantly different. The observed and predicted degradation efficiencies for phenol using NCDA and NCDB catalysts are compared in Table 3 .

Regarding the experimental data, the two models which elucidate the behavior of the system are chosen by software. In order to bind the response for Y1 to 100%, which is the maximum phenol degradation, LOGIT transformation with upper limit as 100 was applied [42,43]:
(3)
L o g i t
Y 1
=
ln
Y 1
100 -
Y 1
= 2.95 + 0.54 A + 0.48 B + 3.2 C + 0.11 A B + 0.58 A C + 0.28 B C + 0.13
A 2
- 0.51
B 2
- 1.16
C 2
(4)
Y 2
= 85.73 + 0.27 A + 2.95 B + 39.92 C - 0.87 A B + 0.85 A C + 0.15 B C + 1.22
A 2
- 5.38
B 2
- 31.33
C 2

According to above equations for Y1 and Y2, there are high and positive coefficients in the models for the amount of the catalyst term. These coefficients are considerably large in comparison to the other model terms’ coefficients indicating the big importance of this parameter in both models.

Regarding Eqs. (3) and (4), for both models positive interactions between H2O2 dosage and amount of catalyst (AC) and reaction time and amount of catalyst (BC) on phenol degradation efficiency are observed. However, the interaction between H2O2 dosage and reaction time (AB) is positive for the phenol removal using NCDA catalyst and is negative for NCDB catalyst. An analysis of variance (ANOVA) for phenol degradation is presented in Table 4 and show the main and interaction effects of parameters influencing the degradation efficiency. The goodness of the fit of the both models was tested by the coefficient of determination (R2), and its integral significance was examined through the F-test. The prob>F-values for the Y1 and Y2 responses are lower than 0.05 showing that both models were statistically significant. R2 for both Y1 and Y2 models were found to be 0.96 and 0.98, respectively which implies on predicted and observed degradation efficiencies are accordance to a great extent. From Table 4, the p-values for both lack of fit tests belongs to Y1 and Y2 models were greater than 0.05 and hence the lack of fit values was not significant relative to the pure error.

To examine an adequate approximation of the selected models to the real system, diagnostic plots including normal plot of residuals and predicted versus actual value are used. The normal distribution of the data is necessary to integral analysis of the experimental data. As shown in the normal probability plot (Fig. 4 ), the experimental data fall close to the straight line approximately and therefore suggests the normal distribution of the data. According to the plot of actual versus predicted values it can be concluded that the proposed correlations are valid.

It can be seen from Fig. 5 how the interactions affects the process responses. The mutual interaction plot for AC and BC reveals that by simultaneously increasing the amount of catalyst and reaction time or increasing the amount of catalyst and H2O2 dosage, for both NCDA and NCDB catalysts, an improvement of the degradation efficiency was occurred. Similarly, for the NCDA catalyst, by increasing the H2O2 dosage and reaction time, simultaneously, the degradation efficiency was improved. As illustrated in Fig. 5, in the case of NCDB catalyst, the changes of the degradation efficiency by altering H2O2 dosage was nearly constant (the value of the model coefficient for A is low) but as the reaction time goes up from 17.5 to 20 min results in lessening of the degradation efficiency from 85.7% to 83.98%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the degradation efficiency of phenol
ce:section.ce:para: The change in the degradation efficiency with the variation of the initial pH value was studied by adjusting the initial pH with 0.1 M HCl and NaOH solutions. In order to study the effect of the initial pH, degradation experiments were performed under the optimum conditions obtained according to RSM. As can be seen in Fig. 6 , the oxidative degradation of phenol was highest at pH 3 while lower degradation efficiencies were observed at higher pH values (4–6). In conclusion, the increase of the pH value more than the initial pH of 3 is not favored to the degradation of phenol in the presence of H2O2. Really the existence of phenol is affected by the initial pH value and the molecular form of phenol exists under acidic conditions but by increasing pH, the amount of OH− is increased and hence under these conditions, weak acid phenol will react with OH− (ionic form of OH) which generated in solution [44]. These higher values of pH retarded the production of HO and thereby the degradation efficiency will decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydroxyl radical generation during catalytic experiment
ce:section.ce:para: OH has the powerful oxidative potential and can effectively oxidize many of the organic pollutants to H2O, CO2 and mineral salts via the fast chain reaction [45]. The TA trapping protocol was employed to detect the generation of OH during catalytic oxidation experiment [46]. The fast reaction between non-fluorescent TA, a well-known OH scavenger, and OH produces fluorescent hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA) and the concentration of HTA can be specified through fluorescence measurements [47]. When the solution involving both TA and HTA molecules is exposed to UV light at 310 nm, thus HTA molecules emit light at the wavelength of 425 nm, while TA molecules do not [48]. The fluorescence spectra for exposure of phenol solution to both types of NCDs and H2O2 and H2O2 alone are shown in Fig. 7 . These spectra were obtained after subtracting from the florescence intensity values for pure NCD samples. The amount of OH detected after H2O2 oxidation was extremely low and could be neglected. But when the NCDs catalysts were added to the solution, the concentration of OH was increased. In the case of NCDA, the fluorescence intensity was higher showing the increase of the total amount of OH trapped by TA. These results clearly demonstrate that the NCDs catalysts can produce OH from H2O2 and consequently catalyze the decomposition of phenol.

The oxidation and reduction potentials of the NCDA and NCDB were determined using cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements by a three-electrode electrochemical cell in a previously work [49]. The electro-catalytic ability of the NCDA and NCDB catalysts to the decomposition of H2O2 and generation of oxidizing radicals can be confirmed using CV [50]. From CV results, both NCDA and NCDB exhibit the same electrochemical behavior but the current values for NCDA were higher which indicates the higher electro activity for this catalyst. These observations confirm the higher phenol degradation with NCDA catalyst. Regarding the electro-catalytic ability of the NCDA and NCDB catalysts, it can be concluded that the interaction of catalysts with hydroxyl radicals occurs due to the electron-transfer annihilation.

In order to determine the possibility of re-use of the catalysts, the degradation of the phenol was performed under optimal conditions (H2O2 dosage of 12 mmol, time of 19 min and amount of catalyst of 1000 ppm) by both types of catalysts for four times and the results of the degradation efficiency are shown in Fig. 8 . For this purpose, after each experiment, the solution was centrifuged and the supernatant was separated. The supernatant was washed several times with ethanol and then was stored in an oven until the solution evaporated and the remaining catalyst was reused. From Fig. 8 it can be observed that the degradation efficiency for both catalysts (NCDA and NCDB) were almost the same in all four runs.

The catalytic oxidation process of phenol was modeled using first-order kinetic model (Eq. (5)).
(5)
ln
C
C 0
= - k t

Fig. 9 is the plot of first-order kinetic expression versus time for phenol degradation by NCDA and NCDB in the presence of H2O2. The inset of these figures indicates that the curves of the catalytic phenol degradation were well fitted by the first-order kinetics and the regressions coefficients are high. The apparent degradation rate constants (k) for the both catalysts at four phenol concentrations were determined from the slope of the degradation curves. It can be seen that by increasing the concentration of phenol k decreases slightly, suggesting a decrease in the efficiency of phenol decomposition.

Typically, hydroxyl radicals react with phenol most strongly in the ortho- and para- positions and hence a mixture of the hydroquinone and catechol are formed as a result of the aromatic hydroxylation of phenol. Then further oxidation of these organic intermediates occurs again and finally short chain fatty acids, carbon dioxide and water are produced [51,52]. The HPLC results are shown in Fig. 10 a–c. As observed on HPLC analysis, the degradation of phenol starts quickly and catechol and hydroquinone are formed (Fig. 10b). After 20 min, the concentrations of organic intermediates are at a maximum and phenol is appearing at a very low amount. It should be noted that during the experiment, an unknown product (first peak appeared in the Fig. 10b, c) is also observed and the characteristics of this compound (structure, molecular weight and HPLC parameters) cannot be determined/quantified.

As can be seen from Fig. 10, the degradation of phenol via hydroxylation/oxidation proceeds to yield hydroquinone and catechol. This reaction was carried out and the three compounds in the solution (phenol, hydroquinone and catechol) were quantified in different times and the changes in their amounts are shown in Fig. 11 .

This contribution, to the best of our knowledge, is the first study to confirm that bio-polymer based carbon dots (NCDA and NCDB) can produce hydroxyl radicals from H2O2 and results in the successful degradation of phenol. In the hydrothermal carbonization synthesize of the bio-polymer based NCDs, Gum Tragacanth and Chitosan were applied as a carbon precursor. XRD study confirms the graphitic nature of the as-prepared NCDs. Also, the efficiency of the two NCD catalysts in degradation of phenol was investigated by CCD under RSM. Predictions from the both proposed models and experimental data agreed well and the RSM could specify the most significant process parameters. The identification of OH radicals has been performed by florescence spectroscopy of HTA. Fluorescence spectra showed that both NCDA and NCDB are powerful candidates for the catalytic activation of H2O2. The results also indicate that the rate of hydroxyl radical generation by NCDB was much lower than that by NCDA. Repeated catalytic experiments suggest that the NCD catalysts can be easily recycled as a heterogeneous catalyst with a long catalyst life.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Research Council of Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences (Grant Number: 97007) for the financial support.
